time_dilation.title: Time dilation, from the light clock
notes: At rest, one tick sends light up to the mirror and back — a distance 2L at speed c.
Set the clock moving. In the platform frame the light runs a diagonal; apply Pythagoras to half a tick of length t.
Bring both t-terms to the same side.
Factor out t squared and divide.
Pull out c and recognise t_0. The moving clock's tick is longer by the factor gamma.

length_contraction.title: Length contraction, by rotating the clock
notes: The transverse light-clock tick — already dilated by gamma.
Now lay the clock along the motion, unknown length L. Light chases a fleeing mirror, then meets an approaching one.
Add the two legs.
Both orientations are one clock: the ticks must match.
The moving rod is shorter along its motion by exactly gamma.

simultaneity_offset.title: Leading clocks lag: the synchronization offset
notes: Synchronise by a flash from the midpoint. In the moving frame the rod (length L) slides; light meets the trailing end early, the leading end late.
Solve each meeting time.
The two clocks, set alike at those meetings, now disagree by this much.
Insert the contracted length L = L_0/gamma. Leading clocks lag by gamma v L_0 over c squared.

invariant_interval.title: The invariant interval, and its minus sign
notes: Take two ticks of a clock moving at v. Its own proper time is dilated by gamma.
Square, and substitute one over gamma squared equals one minus beta squared.
But v squared times delta-t squared is just delta-x squared.
So this combination equals c squared times proper-time squared — the same number in every frame. That minus sign is what survives.

muon.title: The muon at sea level
notes: At its proper lifetime a muon should fall only about 0.66 km — far short of the ground.
But it moves at 0.998 c.
In the lab its clock runs slow: it lives about sixteen times longer.
Now it easily reaches sea level. In the muon's own frame the atmosphere is contracted instead — same survival, two stories.
